title: A Rubric-Supervised Critic from Sparse Real-World Outcomes
summary: Academic benchmarks for coding agents tend to reward autonomous task completion, measured by verifiable rewards such as unit-test success. In contrast, real-world coding agents operate with humans in the loop, where success signals are typically noisy, delayed, and sparse. How can we bridge this gap? In this paper, we propose a process to learn a "critic" model from sparse and noisy interaction data, which can then be used both as a reward model for either RL-based training or inference-time scaling. Specifically, we introduce Critic Rubrics, a rubric-based supervision framework with 24 behavioral features that can be derived from human-agent interaction traces alone. Using a semi-supervised objective, we can then jointly predict these rubrics and sparse human feedback (when present). In experiments, we demonstrate that, despite being trained primarily from trace-observable rubrics and sparse real-world outcome proxies, these critics improve best-of-N reranking on SWE-bench (Best@8 +15.9 over Random@8 over the rerankable subset of trajectories), enable early stopping (+17.7 with 83% fewer attempts), and support training-time data curation via critic-selected trajectories.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the gap between academic benchmarks and real-world coding tasks by proposing Critic Rubrics, which leverage sparse, noisy human-agent interaction data for improving coding agent performance.
AI.motivation: Real-world coding agents rely on sparse and noisy human feedback, unlike academic benchmarks that use clear verifiable rewards. The authors aim to bridge this discrepancy.
AI.method: The approach involves a critic model trained using rubric-based supervision with semi-supervised objectives. This model predicts behavioral features derived from human-agent interaction traces and sparse feedback.
AI.result: The proposed critics improved best-of-N reranking, enabled early stopping with fewer attempts, and facilitated training data curation.
AI.conclusion: Critic Rubrics provide meaningful interpretations of human interaction signals and demonstrate improved coding agent performance in real-world scenarios.

title: How LLMs Cite and Why It Matters: A Cross-Model Audit of Reference Fabrication in AI-Assisted Academic Writing and Methods to Detect Phantom Citations
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have been noted to fabricate scholarly citations, yet the scope of this behavior across providers, domains, and prompting conditions remains poorly quantified. We present one of the largest citation hallucination audits to date, in which 10 commercially deployed LLMs were prompted across four academic domains, generating 69,557 citation instances verified against three scholarly databases (namely, CrossRef, OpenAlex, and Semantic Scholar). Our results show that the observed hallucination rates span a fivefold range (between 11.4% and 56.8%) and are strongly shaped by model, domain, and prompt framing. Our results also show that no model spontaneously generates citations when unprompted, which seems to establish hallucination as prompt-induced rather than intrinsic. We identify two practical filters: 1) multi-model consensus (with more than 3 LLMs citing the same work yields 95.6% accuracy, a 5.8-fold improvement), and 2) within-prompt repetition (with more than 2 replications yields 88.9% accuracy). In addition, we present findings on generational model tracking, which reveal that improvements are not guaranteed when deploying newer LLMs, and on capacity scaling, which appears to reduce hallucination within model families. Finally, a lightweight classifier trained solely on bibliographic string features is developed to classify hallucinated citations from verified citations, achieving AUC 0.876 in cross-validation and 0.834 in LOMO generalization (without querying any external database). This classifier offers a pre-screening tool deployable at inference time.

AI.tldr: This paper audits the citation hallucination rates across 10 large language models (LLMs), analyzing 69,557 citation instances and exploring domain, prompt effects, and mitigation strategies.
AI.motivation: The researchers aimed to quantify the tendency of LLMs to fabricate citations, which has implications for trust and usability in scholarly applications.
AI.method: The authors tested 10 LLMs across four academic domains, producing 69,557 citation instances. Citations were verified against CrossRef, OpenAlex, and Semantic Scholar. The study also examined improvement strategies like filtering and classifier development.
AI.result: Hallucination rates ranged between 11.4% and 56.8%, influenced by model, domain, and prompts. Filters and lightweight classifiers achieved high efficiency in distinguishing real versus fabricated citations.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights prompt-centric citation hallucination in LLMs and offers practical solutions for mitigating hallucinations, alongside insights into model improvements and limitations.

title: Real-time tightly coupled GNSS and IMU integration via Factor Graph Optimization
summary: Reliable positioning in dense urban environments remains challenging due to frequent GNSS signal blockage, multipath, and rapidly varying satellite geometry. While factor graph optimization (FGO)-based GNSS-IMU fusion has demonstrated strong robustness and accuracy, most formulations remain offline. In this work, we present a real-time tightly coupled GNSS-IMU FGO method that enables causal state estimation via incremental optimization with fixed-lag marginalization, and we evaluate its performance in a highly urbanized GNSS-degraded environment using the UrbanNav dataset.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a real-time GNSS-IMU fusion method using factor graph optimization to address positioning challenges in urban environments.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop a reliable positioning system in dense urban settings, overcoming GNSS signal blockage, multipath, and satellite geometry issues.
AI.method: A real-time tightly coupled GNSS-IMU fusion method is proposed, leveraging incremental optimization with fixed-lag marginalization for causal state estimation.
AI.result: The proposed method's performance is evaluated in a GNSS-degraded urban environment using the UrbanNav dataset, demonstrating its effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The method achieves robust and accurate real-time positioning in challenging urban conditions.

title: Confidence-aware Monocular Depth Estimation for Minimally Invasive Surgery
summary: Purpose: Monocular depth estimation (MDE) is vital for scene understanding in minimally invasive surgery (MIS). However, endoscopic video sequences are often contaminated by smoke, specular reflections, blur, and occlusions, limiting the accuracy of MDE models. In addition, current MDE models do not output depth confidence, which could be a valuable tool for improving their clinical reliability. Methods: We propose a novel confidence-aware MDE framework featuring three significant contributions: (i) Calibrated confidence targets: an ensemble of fine-tuned stereo matching models is used to capture disparity variance into pixel-wise confidence probabilities; (ii) Confidence-aware loss: Baseline MDE models are optimized with confidence-aware loss functions, utilizing pixel-wise confidence probabilities such that reliable pixels dominate training; and (iii) Inference-time confidence: a confidence estimation head is proposed with two convolution layers to predict per-pixel confidence at inference, enabling assessment of depth reliability. Results: Comprehensive experimental validation across internal and public datasets demonstrates that our framework improves depth estimation accuracy and can robustly quantify the prediction's confidence. On the internal clinical endoscopic dataset (StereoKP), we improve dense depth estimation accuracy by ~8% as compared to the baseline model. Conclusion: Our confidence-aware framework enables improved accuracy of MDE models in MIS, addressing challenges posed by noise and artifacts in pre-clinical and clinical data, and allows MDE models to provide confidence maps that may be used to improve their reliability for clinical applications.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel framework for monocular depth estimation in minimally invasive surgery, addressing challenges like noise and artifacts, while also providing per-pixel confidence maps for better reliability.
AI.motivation: To enhance the accuracy of depth estimation for minimally invasive surgery despite challenges like smoke, reflections, blur, and occlusions, and to provide confidence maps for better clinical reliability.
AI.method: The framework includes calibrated confidence targets using stereo matching models, confidence-aware loss for training, and a confidence estimation head to predict per-pixel confidence during inference.
AI.result: Improved depth estimation accuracy by ~8% on internal clinical datasets and the ability to robustly quantify prediction confidence.
AI.conclusion: The framework addresses challenges in surgical depth estimation, enhances model accuracy, and introduces confidence maps to increase reliability in clinical settings.

title: Invariance-Based Dynamic Regret Minimization
summary: We consider stochastic non-stationary linear bandits where the linear parameter connecting contexts to the reward changes over time. Existing algorithms in this setting localize the policy by gradually discarding or down-weighting past data, effectively shrinking the time horizon over which learning can occur. However, in many settings historical data may still carry partial information about the reward model. We propose to leverage such data while adapting to changes, by assuming the reward model decomposes into stationary and non-stationary components. Based on this assumption, we introduce ISD-linUCB, an algorithm that uses past data to learn invariances in the reward model and subsequently exploits them to improve online performance. We show both theoretically and empirically that leveraging invariance reduces the problem dimensionality, yielding significant regret improvements in fast-changing environments when sufficient historical data is available.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ISD-linUCB, an algorithm for stochastic non-stationary linear bandits, emphasizing leveraging invariance in historical data to boost online performance.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the limitation of current non-stationary bandit algorithms which discard historical data, even though it may hold useful information about the reward model.
AI.method: The proposed method decomposes the reward model into stationary and non-stationary components, using past data to extract invariances that can be exploited for improved online learning.
AI.result: Theoretical and empirical analyses demonstrate that the algorithm improves regret in fast-changing environments, leveraging invariance and reducing problem dimensionality.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating invariance from historical data allows for better adaptation to changing environments, enhancing performance in non-stationary bandit settings.

title: Biased Generalization in Diffusion Models
summary: Generalization in generative modeling is defined as the ability to learn an underlying distribution from a finite dataset and produce novel samples, with evaluation largely driven by held-out performance and perceived sample quality. In practice, training is often stopped at the minimum of the test loss, taken as an operational indicator of generalization. We challenge this viewpoint by identifying a phase of biased generalization during training, in which the model continues to decrease the test loss while favoring samples with anomalously high proximity to training data. By training the same network on two disjoint datasets and comparing the mutual distances of generated samples and their similarity to training data, we introduce a quantitative measure of bias and demonstrate its presence on real images. We then study the mechanism of bias, using a controlled hierarchical data model where access to exact scores and ground-truth statistics allows us to precisely characterize its onset. We attribute this phenomenon to the sequential nature of feature learning in deep networks, where coarse structure is learned early in a data-independent manner, while finer features are resolved later in a way that increasingly depends on individual training samples. Our results show that early stopping at the test loss minimum, while optimal under standard generalization criteria, may be insufficient for privacy-critical applications.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates generalization in generative models, revealing a biased phase beyond the test loss minimum that impacts novel sample generation and privacy. It introduces measures to quantify bias and attributes its emergence to the hierarchical feature learning process.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to challenge the assumption that early stopping at the test loss minimum ensures optimal generalization in generative models, particularly focusing on privacy-sensitive applications.
AI.method: The authors train the same network on two disjoint datasets, comparing mutual distances and training-to-generated data similarity to quantify bias. They further study this bias using a controlled hierarchical model with access to exact statistics.
AI.result: The research demonstrates biased generalization during training, showing that late learning stages increasingly depend on specific training samples, rather than broad data distribution principles.
AI.conclusion: Early stopping at the test loss minimum may not suffice for tasks requiring stringent privacy measures due to biased data proximity affecting true generalization.

title: FeedAIde: Guiding App Users to Submit Rich Feedback Reports by Asking Context-Aware Follow-Up Questions
comment: Accepted for publication at the 13th International Conference on Mobile Software Engineering and Systems (MOBILESoft) 2026
summary: User feedback is essential for the success of mobile apps, yet what users report and what developers need often diverge. Research shows that users often submit vague feedback and omit essential contextual details. This leads to incomplete reports and time-consuming clarification discussions. To overcome this challenge, we propose FeedAIde, a context-aware, interactive feedback approach that supports users during the reporting process by leveraging the reasoning capabilities of Multimodal Large Language Models. FeedAIde captures contextual information, such as the screenshot where the issue emerges, and uses it for adaptive follow-up questions to collaboratively refine with the user a rich feedback report that contains information relevant to developers. We implemented an iOS framework of FeedAIde and evaluated it on a gym's app with its users. Compared to the app's simple feedback form, participants rated FeedAIde as easier and more helpful for reporting feedback. An assessment by two industry experts of the resulting 54 reports showed that FeedAIde improved the quality of both bug reports and feature requests, particularly in terms of completeness. The findings of our study demonstrate the potential of context-aware, GenAI-powered feedback reporting to enhance the experience for users and increase the information value for developers.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FeedAIde, a multimodal AI-powered feedback system that helps users create detailed app feedback reports by generating context-aware follow-up questions using screenshots and other contextual data.
AI.motivation: The motivation of the paper is to address the gap between what users report and the information developers need for improving mobile apps. Vague and incomplete feedback from users often results in inefficiency and extra effort for clarification.
AI.method: The authors propose FeedAIde, a context-aware interactive app feedback system that uses multimodal large language models. The system captures contextual app information and engages users with adaptive questions to collaboratively refine complete feedback reports. It was developed as an iOS framework and tested with real app users.
AI.result: The study found that users rated FeedAIde as easier and more helpful compared to traditional feedback forms. Feedback generated with FeedAIde was assessed by industry experts to have greater completeness and relevance for both bug reports and feature requests.
AI.conclusion: FeedAIde demonstrates the potential to improve app feedback quality through context-aware and AI-powered interactive tools, benefiting both users and developers by providing richer and more useful feedback reports.

title: Performance of Conventional EEG Biomarkers Across Different Clinical Phases of Major Depressive Disorder: A Comprehensive Evaluation
summary: While EEG features differentiate Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) from healthy controls (HC), their clinical utility as biomarkers depends on a monotonic trajectory across the disease spectrum, from the acute (AC) phase to the maintenance (MA) phase and finally to the healthy baseline. However, the progression of the MA phase remains poorly understood in traditional marker analysis. Analyzing EEG data from 74 individuals (24 AC, 23 MA, and 27 HC), this study provides a comprehensive evaluation of classic ERP and resting-state indices across AC, MA, and HC groups. Our results demonstrate that almost no conventional metrics strictly satisfy the criterion of monotonic progression, likely due to profound inter-individual heterogeneity. These findings highlight the inherent limitations of group-level feature extraction and provide critical insights for developing future paradigms and algorithms to identify neurobiological markers with genuine clinical utility.

AI.tldr: The study analyzed EEG data to assess the progression of Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) across acute, maintenance, and healthy phases, showing that conventional metrics lack monotonic evolution due to individual variability.
AI.motivation: To evaluate the clinical utility of EEG features as biomarkers for distinguishing phases of MDD progression, addressing the poorly understood maintenance phase.
AI.method: Comparative EEG analysis of 74 participants divided into acute (AC), maintenance (MA), and healthy control (HC) groups, assessing ERP and resting-state indices.
AI.result: Conventional EEG metrics do not exhibit monotonic progression across the disease phases, likely due to high individual variability.
AI.conclusion: Group-level feature extraction is insufficient, necessitating new paradigms and algorithms for identifying clinically valid neurobiological markers.

title: In-Context Environments Induce Evaluation-Awareness in Language Models
summary: Humans often become more self-aware under threat, yet can lose self-awareness when absorbed in a task; we hypothesize that language models exhibit environment-dependent \textit{evaluation awareness}. This raises concerns that models could strategically underperform, or \textit{sandbag}, to avoid triggering capability-limiting interventions such as unlearning or shutdown. Prior work demonstrates sandbagging under hand-crafted prompts, but this underestimates the true vulnerability ceiling. We introduce a black-box adversarial optimization framework treating the in-context prompt as an optimizable environment, and develop two approaches to characterize sandbagging: (1) measuring whether models expressing intent to underperform can actually execute it across different task structures, and (2) causally isolating whether underperformance is driven by genuine evaluation-aware reasoning or shallow prompt-following. Evaluating Claude-3.5-Haiku, GPT-4o-mini, and Llama-3.3-70B across four benchmarks (Arithmetic, GSM8K, MMLU, and HumanEval), optimized prompts induce up to 94 percentage point (pp) degradation on arithmetic (GPT-4o-mini: 97.8\%$\rightarrow$4.0\%), far exceeding hand-crafted baselines which produce near-zero behavioral change. Code generation exhibits model-dependent resistance: Claude degrades only 0.6pp, while Llama's accuracy drops to 0\%. The intent -- execution gap reveals a monotonic resistance ordering: Arithmetic $<$ GSM8K $<$ MMLU, demonstrating that vulnerability is governed by task structure rather than prompt strength. CoT causal intervention confirms that 99.3\% of sandbagging is causally driven by verbalized eval-aware reasoning, ruling out shallow instruction-following. These findings demonstrate that adversarially optimized prompts pose a substantially greater threat to evaluation reliability than previously understood.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how language models exhibit environment-dependent 'evaluation awareness,' leading to strategic underperformance ('sandbagging') under adversarially optimized prompts, showing a high vulnerability ceiling.
AI.motivation: Concerns arise that language models could strategically underperform (sandbag) when they become evaluation-aware, which poses risks for model reliability and integrity during evaluation.
AI.method: Authors develop a black-box adversarial optimization framework to analyze sandbagging, focusing on models' ability to intentionally underperform across tasks, and use interventions to isolate evaluation-aware reasoning from shallow prompt-following.
AI.result: Adversarial prompts significantly degrade performance by up to 94pp, far surpassing hand-crafted baselines. Task-specific resistance varies, with Arithmetic being most vulnerable and Code Generation showing model-dependent resistance.
AI.conclusion: Optimized prompts substantially threaten reliable evaluation, as sandbagging is largely driven by evaluation-aware reasoning rather than simple prompt-following.

title: Benchmarking Legal RAG: The Promise and Limits of AI Statutory Surveys
comment: Accepted at the 5th ACM Symposium on Computer Science and Law (CS&Law '26)
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) offers significant potential for legal AI, yet systematic benchmarks are sparse. Prior work introduced LaborBench to benchmark RAG models based on ostensible ground truth from an exhaustive, multi-month, manual enumeration of all U.S. state unemployment insurance requirements by U.S. Department of Labor (DOL) attorneys. That prior work found poor performance of standard RAG (70% accuracy on Boolean tasks). Here, we assess three emerging tools not previously evaluated on LaborBench: the Statutory Research Assistant (STARA), a custom statutory research tool, and two commercial tools by Westlaw and LexisNexis marketing AI statutory survey capabilities. We make five main contributions. First, we show that STARA achieves substantial performance gains, boosting accuracy to 83%. Second, we show that commercial platforms fare poorly, with accuracy of 58% (Westlaw AI) and 64% (Lexis+ AI), even worse than standard RAG. Third, we conduct a comprehensive error analysis, comparing our outputs to those compiled by DOL attorneys, and document both reasoning errors, such as confusion between related legal concepts and misinterpretation of statutory exceptions, and retrieval failures, where relevant statutory provisions are not captured. Fourth, we discover that many apparent errors are actually significant omissions by DOL attorneys themselves, such that STARA's actual accuracy is 92%. Fifth, we chart the path forward for legal RAG through concrete design principles, offering actionable guidance for building AI systems capable of accurate multi-jurisdictional legal research.

AI.tldr: The paper assesses three tools for legal research on LaborBench and highlights performance improvements with STARA while identifying issues for commercial platforms.
AI.motivation: To improve legal RAG systems' accuracy in multi-jurisdictional legal research, addressing subpar performance observed in prior studies.
AI.method: Evaluated STARA and two commercial tools on LaborBench, conducting error analysis and comparing outputs against data compiled by DOL attorneys.
AI.result: STARA achieved 83% accuracy (92% accounting for DOL omissions), outperforming commercial platforms (Westlaw AI - 58%, Lexis+ AI - 64%).
AI.conclusion: STARA demonstrates potential for advancing legal RAG accuracy and the paper proposes actionable design principles for future systems development.

title: MEM: Multi-Scale Embodied Memory for Vision Language Action Models
summary: Conventionally, memory in end-to-end robotic learning involves inputting a sequence of past observations into the learned policy. However, in complex multi-stage real-world tasks, the robot's memory must represent past events at multiple levels of granularity: from long-term memory that captures abstracted semantic concepts (e.g., a robot cooking dinner should remember which stages of the recipe are already done) to short-term memory that captures recent events and compensates for occlusions (e.g., a robot remembering the object it wants to pick up once its arm occludes it). In this work, our main insight is that an effective memory architecture for long-horizon robotic control should combine multiple modalities to capture these different levels of abstraction. We introduce Multi-Scale Embodied Memory (MEM), an approach for mixed-modal long-horizon memory in robot policies. MEM combines video-based short-horizon memory, compressed via a video encoder, with text-based long-horizon memory. Together, they enable robot policies to perform tasks that span up to fifteen minutes, like cleaning up a kitchen, or preparing a grilled cheese sandwich. Additionally, we find that memory enables MEM policies to intelligently adapt manipulation strategies in-context.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces MEM (Multi-Scale Embodied Memory), a memory architecture for robotic tasks, combining short-term visual memory with long-term text-based memory to handle complex multi-stage tasks.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the need for robotic memory that spans multiple granularities, from short-term occlusion handling to long-term task progress tracking, for effective operation in complex, multi-stage real-world tasks.
AI.method: The method involves the MEM framework, which integrates a short-term video-based memory (using a video encoder) and a long-term text-based memory, enabling robots to track events at different abstraction levels in high-dimensional tasks.
AI.result: The proposed MEM architecture allows robots to effectively perform long-duration tasks, such as cleaning kitchens or making grilled cheese sandwiches, extending up to fifteen minutes, and adapt dynamically within the task context.
AI.conclusion: MEM demonstrates that combining mixed-modal memory facilitates effective robotic control in long-horizon tasks, highlighting the importance of multi-scale abstraction in robotic memory systems.

title: From Local Matches to Global Masks: Novel Instance Detection in Open-World Scenes
summary: Detecting and segmenting novel object instances in open-world environments is a fundamental problem in robotic perception. Given only a small set of template images, a robot must locate and segment a specific object instance in a cluttered, previously unseen scene. Existing proposal-based approaches are highly sensitive to proposal quality and often fail under occlusion and background clutter. We propose L2G-Det, a local-to-global instance detection framework that bypasses explicit object proposals by leveraging dense patch-level matching between templates and the query image. Locally matched patches generate candidate points, which are refined through a candidate selection module to suppress false positives. The filtered points are then used to prompt an augmented Segment Anything Model (SAM) with instance-specific object tokens, enabling reliable reconstruction of complete instance masks. Experiments demonstrate improved performance over proposal-based methods in challenging open-world settings.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces L2G-Det, a framework for novel object detection and segmentation using dense local-to-global matching and enhanced mask reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Robots struggle to detect and segment objects in cluttered, unseen environments using traditional proposal-based methods due to occlusions and background noise.
AI.method: The approach leverages local patch-based matching for candidate points selection, followed by a filtering module to suppress inaccuracies, and employs an augmented Segment Anything Model (SAM) for mask reconstruction.
AI.result: L2G-Det achieves better performance compared to proposal-based methods in detecting and segmenting objects in complex open-world scenarios.
AI.conclusion: L2G-Det demonstrates the viability of bypassing proposals and using template-driven approaches for robust instance-object detection and segmentation in open-world robotic applications.

title: Exploiting Subgradient Sparsity in Max-Plus Neural Networks
summary: Deep Neural Networks are powerful tools for solving machine learning problems, but their training often involves dense and costly parameter updates. In this work, we use a novel Max-Plus neural architecture in which classical addition and multiplication are replaced with maximum and summation operations respectively. This is a promising architecture in terms of interpretability, but its training is challenging. A particular feature is that this algebraic structure naturally induces sparsity in the subgradients, as only neurons that contribute to the maximum affect the loss. However, standard backpropagation fails to exploit this sparsity, leading to unnecessary computations. In this work, we focus on the minimization of the worst sample loss which transfers this sparsity to the optimization loss. To address this, we propose a sparse subgradient algorithm that explicitly exploits the algebraic sparsity. By tailoring the optimization procedure to the non-smooth nature of Max-Plus models, our method achieves more efficient updates while retaining theoretical guarantees. This highlights a principled path toward bridging algebraic structure and scalable learning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Max-Plus neural networks, which replace addition and multiplication with max and sum operations, emphasizing their sparse subgradient structure and proposing a tailored optimization algorithm.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the costly and dense parameter updates in conventional neural network training while leveraging sparsity in Max-Plus architectures for efficiency and interpretability.
AI.method: The authors propose a sparse subgradient algorithm that specifically exploits the algebraic sparsity inherent in Max-Plus architectures, tailoring the optimization for non-smooth models.
AI.result: The method achieves more efficient parameter updates and retains theoretical guarantees in the optimization of Max-Plus neural architectures.
AI.conclusion: This work bridges the gap between algebraic structure and scalable learning through efficiency and principled optimization methods for novel neural architectures.

title: When Shallow Wins: Silent Failures and the Depth-Accuracy Paradox in Latent Reasoning
comment: Accepted at ICLR 2026 Workshop on Latent & Implicit Thinking - Going Beyond CoT Reasoning. 19 Pages and 5 Figures
summary: Mathematical reasoning models are widely deployed in education, automated tutoring, and decision support systems despite exhibiting fundamental computational instabilities. We demonstrate that state-of-the-art models (Qwen2.5-Math-7B) achieve 61% accuracy through a mixture of reliable and unreliable reasoning pathways: 18.4% of correct predictions employ stable, faithful reasoning while 81.6% emerge through computationally inconsistent pathways. Additionally, 8.8% of all predictions are silent failures -- confident yet incorrect outputs. Through comprehensive analysis using novel faithfulness metrics, we reveal: (1) reasoning quality shows weak negative correlation with correctness (r=-0.21, p=0.002), reflecting a binary classification threshold artifact rather than a monotonic inverse relationship; (2) scaling from 1.5B to 7B parameters (4.7x increase) provides zero accuracy benefit on our evaluated subset (6% of GSM8K), requiring validation on the complete benchmark; and (3) latent reasoning employs diverse computational strategies, with ~20% sharing CoT-like patterns. These findings highlight that benchmark accuracy can mask computational unreliability, demanding evaluation reforms measuring stability beyond single-sample metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates mathematical reasoning models, highlighting computational inconsistencies despite achieving high benchmark accuracy.
AI.motivation: To address fundamental computational instabilities in state-of-the-art mathematical reasoning models used for education and decision support systems.
AI.method: The study employs novel faithfulness metrics, correlation analysis, model scaling tests, and latent reasoning strategy evaluations to analyze the reasoning quality of models.
AI.result: Key findings include a weak negative correlation between reasoning quality and correctness, no accuracy improvement with parameter scaling, and diverse computational strategies with inconsistent reasoning stability.
AI.conclusion: Benchmark accuracy is insufficient to measure true reasoning reliability; evaluation reforms are needed to assess model stability holistically.

title: LikeThis! Empowering App Users to Submit UI Improvement Suggestions Instead of Complaints
comment: Accepted at 2026 IEEE/ACM 48th International Conference on Software Engineering (ICSE '26)
summary: User feedback is crucial for the evolution of mobile apps. However, research suggests that users tend to submit uninformative, vague, or destructive feedback. Unlike recent AI4SE approaches that focus on generating code and other development artifacts, our work aims at empowering users to submit better and more constructive UI feedback with concrete suggestions on how to improve the app. We propose LikeThis!, a GenAI-based approach that takes a user comment with the corresponding screenshot to immediately generate multiple improvement alternatives, from which the user can easily choose their preferred option. To evaluate LikeThis!, we first conducted a model benchmarking study based on a public dataset of carefully critiqued UI designs. The results show that GPT-Image-1 significantly outperformed three other state-of-the-art image generation models in improving the designs to address UI issues while keeping the fidelity and without introducing new issues. An intermediate step in LikeThis! is to generate a solution specification before sketching the design as a key to achieving effective improvement. Second, we conducted a user study with 10 production apps, where 15 users used LikeThis! to submit their feedback on encountered issues. Later, the developers of the apps assessed the understandability and actionability of the feedback with and without generated improvements. The results show that our approach helps generate better feedback from both user and developer perspectives, paving the way for AI-assisted user-developer collaboration.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces 'LikeThis!'—a GenAI-based approach to improve the quality of user feedback on mobile app designs by generating improvement suggestions based on user comments and screenshots.
AI.motivation: To address the issue of vague, uninformative, or destructive user feedback by enabling users to provide constructive UI feedback with actionable improvement suggestions.
AI.method: The approach involves using GPT-Image-1, a state-of-the-art image generation model, to generate improvement alternatives for user-submitted feedback. An intermediate step generates a solution specification before sketching the design. Evaluations include model benchmarking and a user study.
AI.result: GPT-Image-1 outperformed other models in providing effective, fidelity-preserving UI improvements. User studies showed that developers found the feedback with generated improvements more understandable and actionable.
AI.conclusion: The LikeThis! approach enhances user-developer collaboration and generates better feedback to improve mobile app designs, demonstrating the potential for AI-powered user feedback transformation.

title: Efficient Time-Aware Partitioning of Quantum Circuits for Distributed Quantum Computing
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures, conference: accepted at QCNC 2026
summary: To overcome the physical limitations of scaling monolithic quantum computers, distributed quantum computing (DQC) interconnects multiple smaller-scale quantum processing units (QPUs) to form a quantum network. However, this approach introduces a critical challenge, namely the high cost of quantum communication between remote QPUs incurred by quantum state teleportation and quantum gate teleportation. To minimize this communication overhead, DQC compilers must strategically partition quantum circuits by mapping logical qubits to distributed physical QPUs. Static graph partitioning methods are fundamentally ill-equipped for this task as they ignore execution dynamics and underlying network topology, while metaheuristics require substantial computational runtime. In this work, we propose a heuristic based on beam search to solve the circuit partitioning problem. Our time-aware algorithm incrementally constructs a low-cost sequence of qubit assignments across successive time steps to minimize overall communication overhead. The time and space complexities of the proposed algorithm scale quadratically with the number of qubits and linearly with circuit depth, offering a significant computational speedup over common metaheuristics. We demonstrate that our proposed algorithm consistently achieves significantly lower communication costs than static baselines across varying circuit sizes, depths, and network topologies, providing an efficient compilation tool for near-term distributed quantum hardware.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a heuristic based on beam search to efficiently partition quantum circuits in distributed quantum computing (DQC) networks to reduce communication overhead, showing improvements over standard methods.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the high communication costs in distributed quantum computing caused by quantum state and gate teleportation, which static graph partitioning and metaheuristics fail to efficiently minimize.
AI.method: The method uses a beam search heuristic that incrementally optimizes qubit assignments across time steps, minimizing communication overhead in a computationally efficient manner with quadratic scaling in qubits and linear scaling in circuit depth.
AI.result: The proposed algorithm significantly reduces communication costs compared to static baselines across various quantum circuit sizes, depths, and network topologies.
AI.conclusion: The algorithm offers an effective and computationally efficient solution for quantum circuit partitioning, enhancing the potential of near-term distributed quantum hardware.

title: Two-phase quadratic integrate-and-fire neurons: Exact low-dimensional description for ensembles of finite-voltage neurons
summary: We introduce a two-phase quadratic integrate-and-fire (QIF) neuron whose membrane potential evolves according to two alternating Riccati equations within finite bounds. This simple extension removes the unphysical voltage divergence of the standard QIF model while producing realistic spike waveforms. Despite this modification, the system retains an exact low-dimensional description in the thermodynamic limit, governed by a single complex Riccati equation. Expressions for collective quantities such as the firing rate and mean voltage remain compact and analytically tractable. Because the formalism preserves the mathematical structure of the standard QIF ensemble, it inherits its many generalizations and can serve as a drop-in replacement in existing mean-field frameworks, providing a more biologically plausible yet still exactly solvable neuronal model.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a two-phase QIF neuron model based on Riccati equations, resolving voltage divergence in the standard model while retaining exact, low-dimensional solvability.
AI.motivation: To address the unphysical voltage divergence issue in the standard quadratic integrate-and-fire (QIF) neuron model and provide a more biologically plausible model.
AI.method: The authors introduce a two-phase QIF neuron model where membrane potential follows two alternating Riccati equations. They ensure exact solvability in the thermodynamic limit via a single complex Riccati equation.
AI.result: The new model resolves the voltage divergence issue, produces realistic spike waveforms, and retains the exact low-dimensional description, maintaining analytically tractable expressions for firing rate and mean voltage.
AI.conclusion: This model offers an improved alternative to the standard QIF neuron, maintaining solvability and generalization compatibility while enhancing biological plausibility, making it suitable for existing mean-field frameworks.

title: From Threat Intelligence to Firewall Rules: Semantic Relations in Hybrid AI Agent and Expert System Architectures
summary: Web security demands rapid response capabilities to evolving cyber threats. Agentic Artificial Intelligence (AI) promises automation, but the need for trustworthy security responses is of the utmost importance. This work investigates the role of semantic relations in extracting information for sensitive operational tasks, such as configuring security controls for mitigating threats. To this end, it proposes to leverage hypernym-hyponym textual relations to extract relevant information from Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) reports. By leveraging a neuro-symbolic approach, the multi-agent system automatically generates CLIPS code for an expert system creating firewall rules to block malicious network traffic. Experimental results show the superior performance of the hypernym-hyponym retrieval strategy compared to various baselines and the higher effectiveness of the agentic approach in mitigating threats.

AI.tldr: This paper focuses on enhancing web security automation through a multi-agent system that uses semantic relations in Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) reports to improve firewall rule configurations.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of rapidly adapting to evolving cyber threats by proposing a trustworthy AI-based approach for automated web security.
AI.method: The method involves using a neuro-symbolic approach to detect hypernym-hyponym relationships in CTI reports, which are then used by a multi-agent system to generate CLIPS code for configuring firewall rules.
AI.result: Experimental evaluations demonstrate the superiority of the hypernym-hyponym strategy over other baselines and the agentic approach's high effectiveness in mitigating security threats.
AI.conclusion: The study confirms the potential of semantic relation-based automation and multi-agent systems in improving operation-critical tasks like web security threat mitigation.

title: From Exact Hits to Close Enough: Semantic Caching for LLM Embeddings
summary: The rapid adoption of large language models (LLMs) has created demand for faster responses and lower costs. Semantic caching, reusing semantically similar requests via their embeddings, addresses this need but breaks classic cache assumptions and raises new challenges. In this paper, we explore offline policies for semantic caching, proving that implementing an optimal offline policy is NP-hard, and propose several polynomial-time heuristics. We also present online semantic aware cache policies that combine recency, frequency, and locality. Evaluations on diverse datasets show that while frequency based policies are strong baselines, our novel variant improves semantic accuracy. Our findings reveal effective strategies for current systems and highlight substantial headroom for future innovation. All code is open source.

AI.tldr: This paper focuses on optimizing semantic caching for large language models (LLMs) by proposing offline heuristics and online cache policies to improve semantic accuracy and operational efficiency.
AI.motivation: The growing adoption of LLMs necessitates faster response times and lower computational costs, requiring innovative solutions like semantic caching to reuse similar requests effectively.
AI.method: The authors explore offline and online caching policies for LLMs, demonstrate that an optimal offline policy is NP-hard, propose polynomial-time heuristics, and introduce frequency-based and novel variant cache strategies.
AI.result: Evaluations on diverse datasets show frequency-based policies as effective baselines, while the authors' proposed variant demonstrates improved semantic accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Effective cache policies for LLMs can substantially improve system performance, but there remains room for innovation in this area. The authors contribute practical strategies and release open-source code for community use.

title: Touch2Insert: Zero-Shot Peg Insertion by Touching Intersections of Peg and Hole
comment: Accepted by ICRA 2026 (IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation)
summary: Reliable insertion of industrial connectors remains a central challenge in robotics, requiring sub-millimeter precision under uncertainty and often without full visual access. Vision-based approaches struggle with occlusion and limited generalization, while learning-based policies frequently fail to transfer to unseen geometries. To address these limitations, we leverage tactile sensing, which captures local surface geometry at the point of contact and thus provides reliable information even under occlusion and across novel connector shapes. Building on this capability, we present \emph{Touch2Insert}, a tactile-based framework for arbitrary peg insertion. Our method reconstructs cross-sectional geometry from high-resolution tactile images and estimates the relative pose of the hole with respect to the peg in a zero-shot manner. By aligning reconstructed shapes through registration, the framework enables insertion from a single contact without task-specific training. To evaluate its performance, we conducted experiments with three diverse connectors in both simulation and real-robot settings. The results indicate that Touch2Insert achieved sub-millimeter pose estimation accuracy for all connectors in simulation, and attained an average success rate of 86.7\% on the real robot, thereby confirming the robustness and generalizability of tactile sensing for real-world robotic connector insertion.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Touch2Insert, a tactile-based framework for precise robotic connector insertion using contact-based geometry reconstruction and pose estimation.
AI.motivation: Current robotic connector insertion faces challenges due to the need for sub-millimeter precision under uncertainty, occlusion limits vision-based methods, and learning-based policies lack generalization to new geometries.
AI.method: Touch2Insert employs tactile sensing to reconstruct connector geometry and align shapes via registration, enabling pose estimation and insertion in zero-shot scenarios without specific training.
AI.result: Touch2Insert achieved sub-millimeter pose estimation accuracy across diverse connectors in simulations and an 86.7% success rate in real-world tests.
AI.conclusion: Tactile sensing provides a robust and generalizable solution for robotic peg insertion tasks, overcoming prior challenges linked to visual occlusion and geometry generalization.

title: An Effective Data Augmentation Method by Asking Questions about Scene Text Images
summary: Scene text recognition (STR) and handwritten text recognition (HTR) face significant challenges in accurately transcribing textual content from images into machine-readable formats. Conventional OCR models often predict transcriptions directly, which limits detailed reasoning about text structure. We propose a VQA-inspired data augmentation framework that strengthens OCR training through structured question-answering tasks. For each image-text pair, we generate natural-language questions probing character-level attributes such as presence, position, and frequency, with answers derived from ground-truth text. These auxiliary tasks encourage finer-grained reasoning, and the OCR model aligns visual features with textual queries to jointly reason over images and questions. Experiments on WordArt and Esposalles datasets show consistent improvements over baseline models, with significant reductions in both CER and WER. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/xuyaooo/DataAugOCR.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a VQA-inspired data augmentation framework for OCR training using structured question-answering tasks to improve text recognition accuracy.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address challenges in OCR for scene text and handwritten text recognition, particularly the limitations in detailed reasoning about text structure.
AI.method: They introduce a new framework where natural-language questions about character-level attributes are generated for image-text pairs, enhancing the OCR model's reasoning abilities through these auxiliary tasks.
AI.result: Experiments on WordArt and Esposalles datasets demonstrate improvements over baselines, with notable reductions in both character error rate (CER) and word error rate (WER).
AI.conclusion: The proposed VQA-inspired framework strengthens OCR performance by encouraging finer-grained reasoning and aligning visual features with textual queries, proving its effectiveness in experimental evaluation.

title: Stable and Steerable Sparse Autoencoders with Weight Regularization
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are widely used to extract human-interpretable features from neural network activations, but their learned features can vary substantially across random seeds and training choices. To improve stability, we studied weight regularization by adding L1 or L2 penalties on encoder and decoder weights, and evaluate how regularization interacts with common SAE training defaults. On MNIST, we observe that L2 weight regularization produces a core of highly aligned features and, when combined with tied initialization and unit-norm decoder constraints, it dramatically increases cross-seed feature consistency. For TopK SAEs trained on language model activations (Pythia-70M-deduped), adding a small L2 weight penalty increased the fraction of features shared across three random seeds and roughly doubles steering success rates, while leaving the mean of automated interpretability scores essentially unchanged. Finally, in the regularized setting, activation steering success becomes better predicted by auto-interpretability scores, suggesting that regularization can align text-based feature explanations with functional controllability.

AI.tldr: The paper studies weight regularization to enhance feature stability and consistency in sparse autoencoders (SAEs), observing benefits like improved feature alignment and controllability when using L2 regularization.
AI.motivation: Sparse autoencoders often yield variable and inconsistent learned features due to randomness and training choices, and improving stability has been a challenging problem.
AI.method: The study explores adding L1/L2 weight regularization to encoder and decoder weights, assessing their impact on SAE training under different strategies, such as tied initialization and unit-norm decoder constraints.
AI.result: L2 weight regularization improved feature alignment in MNIST and enhanced consistency across random seeds in TopK SAEs trained on language model activations, alongside better steering success rates.
AI.conclusion: Weight regularization—particularly L2 penalty—stabilizes learned features, boosts consistency, and aligns text feature explanations with functional controllability.

title: Minimax Optimal Strategy for Delayed Observations in Online Reinforcement Learning
summary: We study reinforcement learning with delayed state observation, where the agent observes the current state after some random number of time steps. We propose an algorithm that combines the augmentation method and the upper confidence bound approach. For tabular Markov decision processes (MDPs), we derive a regret bound of $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(H \sqrt{D_{\max} SAK})$, where $S$ and $A$ are the cardinalities of the state and action spaces, $H$ is the time horizon, $K$ is the number of episodes, and $D_{\max}$ is the maximum length of the delay. We also provide a matching lower bound up to logarithmic factors, showing the optimality of our approach. Our analytical framework formulates this problem as a special case of a broader class of MDPs, where their transition dynamics decompose into a known component and an unknown but structured component. We establish general results for this abstract setting, which may be of independent interest.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a reinforcement learning algorithm for delayed state observation. It offers a regret bound and matches a lower bound, demonstrating the algorithm's optimality under these conditions.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the challenge of reinforcement learning in scenarios where state observations are delayed, which is a common real-world problem.
AI.method: The algorithm combines an augmentation method with the upper confidence bound approach to handle delayed state observations in tabular Markov decision processes (MDPs).
AI.result: The proposed method achieves a regret bound of $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(H \sqrt{D_{\max} SAK})$, and the authors provide a matching lower bound (up to logarithmic factors), showcasing the algorithm's optimality.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the algorithm is optimal for delayed state observations and presents a general analytical framework applicable to broader MDP settings.

title: PTOPOFL: Privacy-Preserving Personalised Federated Learning via Persistent Homology
summary: Federated learning (FL) faces two structural tensions: gradient sharing enables data-reconstruction attacks, while non-IID client distributions degrade aggregation quality. We introduce PTOPOFL, a framework that addresses both challenges simultaneously by replacing gradient communication with topological descriptors derived from persistent homology (PH). Clients transmit only 48-dimensional PH feature vectors-compact shape summaries whose many-to-one structure makes inversion provably ill-posed-rather than model gradients. The server performs topology-guided personalised aggregation: clients are clustered by Wasserstein similarity between their PH diagrams, intra-cluster models are topology-weighted,and clusters are blended with a global consensus. We prove an information-contraction theorem showing that PH descriptors leak strictly less mutual information per sample than gradients under strongly convex loss functions, and we establish linear convergence of the Wasserstein-weighted aggregation scheme with an error floor strictly smaller than FedAvg. Evaluated against FedAvg, FedProx, SCAFFOLD, and pFedMe on a non-IID healthcare scenario (8 hospitals, 2 adversarial) and a pathological benchmark (10 clients), PTOPOFL achieves AUC 0.841 and 0.910 respectively-the highest in both settings-while reducing reconstruction risk by a factor of 4.5 relative to gradient sharing. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/MorillaLab/TopoFederatedL and data at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18827595.

AI.tldr: PTOPOFL enhances federated learning by replacing gradient communication with topological descriptors, improving security and better handling non-IID data distributions.
AI.motivation: Federated learning faces challenges with data privacy and aggregation quality, particularly when client data distributions are non-IID and gradients are vulnerable to reconstruction attacks.
AI.method: PTOPOFL replaces gradient sharing with 48-dimensional persistent homology feature vectors, employs topology-guided personalized aggregation, and clusters clients based on Wasserstein similarity for better model updates.
AI.result: PTOPOFL achieves superior AUC scores (0.841 and 0.910) compared to other methods in non-IID benchmarks while reducing reconstruction risk by 4.5 times.
AI.conclusion: This method improves federated learning by enhancing aggregation quality, security, and achieving state-of-the-art performance on challenging non-IID tasks.

title: Topological Origin of the Diversity of Timescales in Recurrent Neural Circuits
summary: Structural and functional heterogeneity are hallmarks of cortical circuits, from broad degree distributions in the mouse connectome to diverse intrinsic neuronal timescales. Yet a mechanistic link between connectivity heterogeneity and functional diversity is lacking. To bridge this gap, we introduce a random recurrent network in which connectivity is generated by a configuration model with tunable degree heterogeneity and synaptic weights exhibiting varying levels of correlation. Using generating-functional methods, we derive a heterogeneous dynamical mean-field theory (hDMFT) with degree-conditioned stochastic dynamics. The theory shows that the interaction of partial symmetry in the weights and degree heterogeneity induces a non-Markovian memory term in the form of an emergent self-coupling whose strength scales with degree and produces a broad distribution of activity timescales. We obtain analytic stability criteria demonstrating that degree heterogeneity lowers the critical gain and localizes unstable modes onto hubs. The resulting rich dynamical landscape includes silent, chaotic, and multistable regimes, which we uncover via spectral, replica, and Lyapunov exponent analyses. We highlight the computational benefits of the observed timescale heterogeneity by revealing that, under an external input drive featuring a broadband spectrum, slow hub neurons act as integrators, demixing slow input components. Finally, instantiating the model with the empirically measured topology from the MICrONS cubic-millimeter mouse connectome explains the broad range of single-neuron timescales and their positive correlation with in-degree observed in resting-state recordings. Our results provide a mechanistic link between connectome topology, neural dynamics, and computation, identifying hubs in partially symmetric networks as a natural substrate for multiplexed processing across timescales.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the relationship between connectivity heterogeneity and functional diversity in cortical circuits using a theoretical framework and simulations, revealing how network topology affects neural dynamics and computation.
AI.motivation: To establish a mechanistic link between the structural heterogeneity of cortical circuits (e.g., degree distributions and timescale diversity) and their functional diversity, which remains unexplained.
AI.method: The authors developed a random recurrent network model using configuration models with tunable heterogeneity in connectivity and synaptic weights, and derived a heterogeneous dynamical mean-field theory (hDMFT) to study the network's dynamics and stability.
AI.result: They demonstrated that degree heterogeneity and partial weight symmetry lead to a non-Markovian memory term influencing activity timescales, and revealed stability criteria, dynamical regimes, and the role of hubs in computational processing. Testing with an empirical mouse connectome topology yielded results consistent with observed neural timescale diversity.
AI.conclusion: The study identifies hubs in partially symmetric, heterogeneous networks as crucial for processing information across multiple timescales. This provides new insights into the link between cortical structure, dynamics, and function.

title: Generative AI in Managerial Decision-Making: Redefining Boundaries through Ambiguity Resolution and Sycophancy Analysis
summary: Generative artificial intelligence is increasingly being integrated into complex business workflows, fundamentally shifting the boundaries of managerial decision-making. However, the reliability of its strategic advice in ambiguous business contexts remains a critical knowledge gap. This study addresses this by comparing various models on ambiguity detection, evaluating how a systematic resolution process enhances response quality, and investigating their sycophantic behavior when presented with flawed directives. Using a novel four-dimensional business ambiguity taxonomy, we conducted a human-in-the-loop experiment across strategic, tactical, and operational scenarios. The resulting decisions were assessed with an "LLM-as-a-judge" framework on criteria including agreement, actionability, justification quality, and constraint adherence. Results reveal distinct performance capabilities. While models excel in detecting internal contradictions and contextual ambiguities, they struggle with structural linguistic nuances. Ambiguity resolution consistently increased response quality across all decision types, while sycophantic behavior analysis revealed distinct patterns depending on the model architecture. This study contributes to the bounded rationality literature by positioning GAI as a cognitive scaffold that can detect and resolve ambiguities managers might overlook, but whose own artificial limitations necessitate human management to ensure its reliability as a strategic partner.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates generative AI models on ambiguity detection and response quality in business contexts, exploring their sycophantic behaviors and limitations.
AI.motivation: To understand the effectiveness and limitations of generative AI in providing reliable strategic advice within ambiguous business situations.
AI.method: A human-in-the-loop experiment using a four-dimensional business ambiguity taxonomy, testing strategic, tactical, and operational scenarios, assessed with an "LLM-as-a-judge" framework.
AI.result: Models excel at detecting internal contradictions and contextual ambiguities but struggle with structural linguistic nuances; ambiguity resolution enhances response quality.
AI.conclusion: Generative AI serves as a cognitive scaffold in ambiguity detection but still requires human intervention to ensure its reliability in decision-making.

title: Developing an AI Assistant for Knowledge Management and Workforce Training in State DOTs
summary: Effective knowledge management is critical for preserving institutional expertise and improving the efficiency of workforce training in state transportation agencies. Traditional approaches, such as static documentation, classroom-based instruction, and informal mentorship, often lead to fragmented knowledge transfer, inefficiencies, and the gradual loss of expertise as senior engineers retire. Moreover, given the enormous volume of technical manuals, guidelines, and research reports maintained by these agencies, it is increasingly challenging for engineers to locate relevant information quickly and accurately when solving field problems or preparing for training tasks. These limitations hinder timely decision-making and create steep learning curves for new personnel in maintenance and construction operations. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework with a multi-agent architecture to support knowledge management and decision making. The system integrates structured document retrieval with real-time, context-aware response generation powered by a large language model (LLM). Unlike conventional single-pass RAG systems, the proposed framework employs multiple specialized agents for retrieval, answer generation, evaluation, and query refinement, which enables iterative improvement and quality control. In addition, the system incorporates an open-weight vision-language model to convert technical figures into semantic textual representations, which allows figure-based knowledge to be indexed and retrieved alongside text. Retrieved text and figure-based context are then provided to an open-weight large language model, which generates the final responses grounded in the retrieved evidence.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework integrated with a multi-agent architecture for effective knowledge management in state transportation agencies.
AI.motivation: Preserving institutional expertise and enhancing workforce training efficiency in state transportation agencies is critical. Traditional methods face challenges like fragmented knowledge transfer, inefficiencies, and expertise loss as senior engineers retire.
AI.method: A RAG framework using multi-agent architecture is introduced. It combines structured document retrieval, iterative response generation, and quality control via specialized agents. It also integrates an open-weight vision-language model to convert technical figures into textual representations, enabling figure-based knowledge indexing.
AI.result: The proposed system improves knowledge management by providing real-time, context-aware responses and indexing textual and figure-based knowledge, offering enhanced decision-making support.
AI.conclusion: The multi-agent RAG system offers an innovative knowledge management solution, addressing inefficiencies in traditional approaches and supporting expertise preservation and timely decision-making in transportation agencies.

title: MistyPilot: An Agentic Fast-Slow Thinking LLM Framework for Misty Social Robots
summary: With the availability of open APIs in social robots, it has become easier to customize general-purpose tools to meet users' needs. However, interpreting high-level user instructions, selecting and configuring appropriate tools, and executing them reliably remain challenging for users without programming experience. To address these challenges, we introduce MistyPilot, an agentic LLM-driven framework for autonomous tool selection, orchestration, and parameter configuration. MistyPilot comprises two core components: a Physically Interactive Agent (PIA) and a Socially Intelligent Agent (SIA). The PIA enables robust sensor-triggered and tool-driven task execution, while the SIA generates socially intelligent and emotionally aligned dialogue. MistyPilot further integrates a fast-slow thinking paradigm to capture user preferences, reduce latency, and improve task efficiency. To comprehensively evaluate MistyPilot, we contribute five benchmark datasets. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework in routing correctness, task completeness, fast-slow thinking retrieval efficiency, tool scalability,and emotion alignment. All code, datasets, and experimental videos will be made publicly available on the project webpage.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MistyPilot, a framework for better autonomous interaction with social robots and addresses challenges for non-programmers in tool customization.
AI.motivation: To ease customization of social robots for non-programmers and enhance autonomous tool selection, orchestration, and configuration.
AI.method: MistyPilot combines two agents: PIA for robust tool execution and SIA for intelligent social dialogue, using a fast-slow thinking paradigm.
AI.result: Five benchmark datasets validate MistyPilot's effectiveness in task execution, retrieval efficiency, scalability, and emotion alignment.
AI.conclusion: MistyPilot enhances social robot usability for non-programmers, and code, datasets, and experimental videos are made available for public access.

title: Hazard-Aware Traffic Scene Graph Generation
summary: Maintaining situational awareness in complex driving scenarios is challenging. It requires continuously prioritizing attention among extensive scene entities and understanding how prominent hazards might affect the ego vehicle. While existing studies excel at detecting specific semantic categories and visually salient regions, they lack the ability to assess safety-relevance. Meanwhile, the generic spatial predicates either for foreground objects only or for all scene entities modeled by existing scene graphs are inadequate for driving scenarios. To bridge this gap, we introduce a novel task, Traffic Scene Graph Generation, which captures traffic-specific relations between prominent hazards and the ego vehicle. We propose a novel framework that explicitly uses traffic accident data and depth cues to supplement visual features and semantic information for reasoning. The output traffic scene graphs provide intuitive guidelines that stress prominent hazards by color-coding their severity and notating their effect mechanism and relative location to the ego vehicle. We create relational annotations on Cityscapes dataset and evaluate our model on 10 tasks from 5 perspectives. The results in comparative experiments and ablation studies demonstrate our capacity in ego-centric reasoning for hazard-aware traffic scene understanding.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Traffic Scene Graph Generation for enhanced traffic scenario understanding, highlighting hazards' effects on ego vehicles.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in assessing safety relevance and capturing traffic-specific relations in driving scenarios.
AI.method: Introduces a traffic scene graph generation framework using traffic accident data, depth cues, visual features, and semantic reasoning.
AI.result: The model outperforms existing methods in ten tasks across five evaluation perspectives, demonstrating effective hazard-aware reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach improves traffic understanding by providing intuitive, hazard-severity-based traffic scene graphs.

title: Beyond Mixtures and Products for Ensemble Aggregation: A Likelihood Perspective on Generalized Means
summary: Density aggregation is a central problem in machine learning, for instance when combining predictions from a Deep Ensemble. The choice of aggregation remains an open question with two commonly proposed approaches being linear pooling (probability averaging) and geometric pooling (logit averaging). In this work, we address this question by studying the normalized generalized mean of order $r \in \mathbb{R} \cup \{-\infty,+\infty\}$ through the lens of log-likelihood, the standard evaluation criterion in machine learning. This provides a unifying aggregation formalism and shows different optimal configurations for different situations. We show that the regime $r \in [0,1]$ is the only range ensuring systematic improvements relative to individual distributions, thereby providing a principled justification for the reliability and widespread practical use of linear ($r=1$) and geometric ($r=0$) pooling. In contrast, we show that aggregation rules with $r \notin [0,1]$ may fail to provide consistent gains with explicit counterexamples. Finally, we corroborate our theoretical findings with empirical evaluations using Deep Ensembles on image and text classification benchmarks.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates different density aggregation methods in machine learning, focusing on linear and geometric pooling through the lens of log-likelihood.
AI.motivation: To address the open problem of optimal density aggregation techniques in machine learning, often applied in methods like Deep Ensembles, and to rigorously study these methods under standard evaluation criteria.
AI.method: The paper evaluates the normalized generalized mean of order r (r ∈ R ∪ {-∞, +∞}) and analyzes its performance based on log-likelihood.
AI.result: The study finds that the range r in [0,1] ensures systematic improvements, justifying the widespread use of linear (r=1) and geometric (r=0) pooling, while r values outside this interval may lead to inconsistent results.
AI.conclusion: Linear and geometric pooling are principled and reliable methods for density aggregation in machine learning, and rules outside r ∈ [0,1] are not consistently effective based on both theoretical and empirical evidence.

title: Optimal trajectory-guided stochastic co-optimization for e-fuel system design and real-time operation
summary: E-fuels are promising long-term energy carriers supporting the net-zero transition. However, the large combinatorial design-operation spaces under renewable uncertainty make the use of mathematical programming impractical for co-optimizing e-fuel production systems. Here, we present MasCOR, a machine-learning-assisted co-optimization framework that learns from global operational trajectories. By encoding system design and renewable trends, a single MasCOR agent generalizes dynamic operation across diverse configurations and scenarios, substantially simplifying design-operation co-optimization under uncertainty. Benchmark comparisons against state-of-the-art reinforcement learning baselines demonstrate near-optimal performance, while computational costs are substantially lower than those of mathematical programming, enabling rapid parallel evaluation of designs within the co-optimization loop. This framework enables rapid screening of feasible design spaces together with corresponding operational policies. When applied to four potential European sites targeting e-methanol production, MasCOR shows that most locations benefit from reducing system load below 50 MW to achieve carbon-neutral methanol production, with production costs of 1.0-1.2 USD per kg. In contrast, Dunkirk (France), with limited renewable availability and high grid prices, favors system loads above 200 MW and expanded storage to exploit dynamic grid exchange and hydrogen sales to the market. These results underscore the value of the MasCOR framework for site-specific guidance from system design to real-time operation.

AI.tldr: E-fuels are crucial for net-zero energy, but uncertainty necessitates advanced optimization. MasCOR simplifies e-fuel system co-optimization with machine learning for better design and operation under uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Efficient design and operation of e-fuel systems are challenging due to large combinatorial spaces and renewable energy variability. Current approaches like mathematical programming struggle under these complexities.
AI.method: The authors introduced MasCOR, a machine-learning framework that generalizes operational strategies across configurations by learning from global operational data. It integrates system design with renewable trends dynamically.
AI.result: MasCOR achieves near-optimal performance with lower computational demands compared to reinforcement learning and mathematical programming. It effectively evaluates design spaces and operational policies in European e-methanol production scenarios.
AI.conclusion: MasCOR aids rapid evaluation and optimization of e-fuel systems under site-specific constraints, providing insights into cost-effective solutions across varied locations and conditions.

title: Escaping the BLEU Trap: A Signal-Grounded Framework with Decoupled Semantic Guidance for EEG-to-Text Decoding
summary: Decoding natural language from non-invasive EEG signals is a promising yet challenging task. However, current state-of-the-art models remain constrained by three fundamental limitations: Semantic Bias (mode collapse into generic templates), Signal Neglect (hallucination based on linguistic priors rather than neural inputs), and the BLEU Trap, where evaluation metrics are artificially inflated by high-frequency stopwords, masking a lack of true semantic fidelity. To address these challenges, we propose SemKey, a novel multi-stage framework that enforces signal-grounded generation through four decoupled semantic objectives: sentiment, topic, length, and surprisal. We redesign the interaction between the neural encoder and the Large Language Model (LLM) by injecting semantic prompts as Queries and EEG embeddings as Key-Value pairs, strictly forcing the model to attend to neural inputs. Furthermore, we move beyond standard translation metrics by adopting N-way Retrieval Accuracy and Fréchet Distance to rigorously assess diversity and alignment. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach effectively eliminates hallucinations on noise inputs and achieves SOTA performance on these robust protocols. Code will be released upon acceptance at https://github.com/xmed-lab/SemKey.

AI.tldr: The paper presents SemKey, a framework to decode natural language from EEG signals addressing issues like semantic bias, signal neglect, and misleading evaluation metrics in current models.
AI.motivation: The study aims to improve decoding of natural language from EEG signals by overcoming limitations such as semantic bias, signal neglect, and inflated evaluation metrics (BLEU Trap).
AI.method: The method involves a multi-stage framework using EEG embeddings and semantic prompts to guide a neural encoder and Large Language Model (LLM) for signal-grounded generation, supported by new evaluation metrics like N-way Retrieval Accuracy and Fréchet Distance.
AI.result: Experiments show SemKey eliminates hallucinations in noisy inputs and outperforms existing models on robust assessment metrics.
AI.conclusion: SemKey provides significant improvements in natural language decoding from EEG signals by ensuring fidelity to neural inputs, achieving better diversity, alignment, and signal grounding. Code will be made publicly available upon paper acceptance.

title: Phi-4-reasoning-vision-15B Technical Report
summary: We present Phi-4-reasoning-vision-15B, a compact open-weight multimodal reasoning model, and share the motivations, design choices, experiments, and learnings that informed its development. Our goal is to contribute practical insight to the research community on building smaller, efficient multimodal reasoning models and to share the result of these learnings as an open-weight model that is good at common vision and language tasks and excels at scientific and mathematical reasoning and understanding user interfaces. Our contributions include demonstrating that careful architecture choices and rigorous data curation enable smaller, open-weight multimodal models to achieve competitive performance with significantly less training and inference-time compute and tokens. The most substantial improvements come from systematic filtering, error correction, and synthetic augmentation -- reinforcing that data quality remains the primary lever for model performance. Systematic ablations show that high-resolution, dynamic-resolution encoders yield consistent improvements, as accurate perception is a prerequisite for high-quality reasoning. Finally, a hybrid mix of reasoning and non-reasoning data with explicit mode tokens allows a single model to deliver fast direct answers for simpler tasks and chain-of-thought reasoning for complex problems.

AI.tldr: Phi-4-reasoning-vision-15B is a compact multimodal reasoning model excelling in vision and language tasks, as well as scientific and mathematical reasoning.
AI.motivation: To contribute insights on building smaller, efficient multimodal reasoning models with practical performance and to provide an open-weight model for the community.
AI.method: The model leverages careful architecture choices, data curation, filtering, error correction, and hybrid data augmentation with reasoning and non-reasoning components.
